[[JA:二宮神社 (三宅村)]]

{{wikidata link|Q134930277}}

== Japanese content ==
{{神社
|名称   = 后神社
|画像= {{画像募集中|cat=東京都島嶼部}}
|所在地 = {{jalink|東京都|Q1490}}{{jalink|三宅村|Q1154797}}{{jalink|坪田村|Q11425774|坪田}}
|位置 = {{Coord|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E|display=inline,title}}
|祭神   = 伊波乃比咩命
|社格   = {{jalink|式内社}}
|創建   = 不詳
|本殿   = 
|別名   = 
|札所等 = 
|例祭   = 
|神事   = 
|地図 = {{Location map|Japan|label=二宮神社|coordinates=
{{coord2|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E}}}}
}}
'''二宮神社'''（にのみやじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|東京都|Q1490}}{{jalink|三宅村|Q1154797}}（{{jalink|三宅島|Q1075076}}）にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。

三宅島南東の港・坪田(つぼた)漁港の目前に鎮座している。

'''伊波乃比咩命神社'''（いわのひめのみことじんじゃ）を中心に{{jalink|式内社}}数社、その他周辺小社が数多く合祀されている<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - 三宅島観光協会</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - 延喜式神社の調査</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf 三宅島 p.84 (三宅島の式内社)] - 東京都/三宅島観光協会</ref>。

三宅島内にある{{jalink|事代主神}}（三島大明神）の后を祀る3つの神社の内の1つ<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html 歴史] - 東京都三宅島</ref>。

=== 歴史 ===
中心となっている伊波乃比咩命神社は、元は現在地の北方山中（現・伊波耶観音神社 (坪田観音)）にあり、その後、鎌倉初期に現在地の南西・大路池南方（小倉山、鳥居現存）に遷座し、その後、{{jalink|昭和|Q327567}}29年（{{jalink|1954年|Q18581}}）に現在地に鎮座していた{{jalink|天神信仰|Q66890725}}の{{jalink|菅原神社|Q48532247}}に周辺神社を集約して合祀し、二宮神社と改名した<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>。

二宮神社の名の由来は、祭神が箱根三姉妹の次女だからとも解釈できるが、公式には祭神が伴っていたと伝承される次男王子に由来すると説明されている<ref name="kanko"/>。

=== 祭神 ===
* 伊波乃比咩命（いわのひめのみこと） - {{jalink|事代主神}}（三島大明神）の后神の一柱。

『三宅記』の神話によると、三宅島の神社に祀られている三島大明神（事代主神）の三姉妹の后は、{{jalink|箱根|Q11603342}}{{jalink|芦ノ湖|Q187053}}周辺に住んでいた翁媼（老夫婦）の娘たちを、龍神（大蛇）から救って三宅島に迎えたものであり、長女が{{jalink|后神社 (三宅村)|Q134930558|后神社}}に祀られている伊賀牟比売命、次女が二宮神社に祀られている伊波乃比咩命、三女が{{jalink|御笏神社|Q110915859}}に祀られている佐伎多麻比咩命とされる<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html 三宅島の神社] - 延喜式神社の調査</ref>。

== Japanese content ==

{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E}}}}
}}
'''二宮神社'''（にのみやじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|東京都|Q1490}}{{jalink|三宅村|Q1154797}}（{{jalink|三宅島|Q1075076}}）にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。

三宅島南東の港・坪田(つぼた)漁港の目前に鎮座している。

'''伊波乃比咩命神社'''（いわのひめのみことじんじゃ）を中心に{{jalink|式内社}}数社、その他周辺小社が数多く合祀されている<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - 三宅島観光協会</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - 延喜式神社の調査</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf 三宅島 p.84 (三宅島の式内社)] - 東京都/三宅島観光協会</ref>。

三宅島内にある{{jalink|事代主神}}（三島大明神）の后を祀る3つの神社の内の1つ<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html 歴史] - 東京都三宅島</ref>。

=== 歴史 ===
中心となっている伊波乃比咩命神社は、元は現在地の北方山中（現・伊波耶観音神社 (坪田観音)）にあり、その後、鎌倉初期に現在地の南西・大路池南方（小倉山、鳥居現存）に遷座し、その後、{{jalink|昭和|Q327567}}29年（{{jalink|1954年|Q18581}}）に現在地に鎮座していた{{jalink|天神信仰|Q66890725}}の{{jalink|菅原神社|Q48532247}}に周辺神社を集約して合祀し、二宮神社と改名した<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>。

二宮神社の名の由来は、祭神が箱根三姉妹の次女だからとも解釈できるが、公式には祭神が伴っていたと伝承される次男王子に由来すると説明されている<ref name="kanko"/>。

=== 祭神 ===
* 伊波乃比咩命（いわのひめのみこと） - {{jalink|事代主神}}（三島大明神）の后神の一柱。

『三宅記』の神話によると、三宅島の神社に祀られている三島大明神（事代主神）の三姉妹の后は、{{jalink|箱根|Q11603342}}{{jalink|芦ノ湖|Q187053}}周辺に住んでいた翁媼（老夫婦）の娘たちを、龍神（大蛇）から救って三宅島に迎えたものであり、長女が{{jalink|后神社 (三宅村)|Q134930558|后神社}}に祀られている伊賀牟比売命、次女が二宮神社に祀られている伊波乃比咩命、三女が{{jalink|御笏神社|Q110915859}}に祀られている佐伎多麻比咩命とされる<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html 三宅島の神社] - 延喜式神社の調査</ref>。

'''二宮神社'''（にのみやじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|東京都|Q1490}}{{jalink|三宅村|Q1154797}}（{{jalink|三宅島|Q1075076}}）にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。

三宅島南東の港・坪田(つぼた)漁港の目前に鎮座している。

'''伊波乃比咩命神社'''（いわのひめのみことじんじゃ）を中心に{{jalink|式内社}}数社、その他周辺小社が数多く合祀されている<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - 三宅島観光協会</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - 延喜式神社の調査</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf 三宅島 p.84 (三宅島の式内社)] - 東京都/三宅島観光協会</ref>。

三宅島内にある{{jalink|事代主神}}（三島大明神）の后を祀る3つの神社の内の1つ<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html 歴史] - 東京都三宅島</ref>。

=== 歴史 ===
中心となっている伊波乃比咩命神社は、元は現在地の北方山中（現・伊波耶観音神社 (坪田観音)）にあり、その後、鎌倉初期に現在地の南西・大路池南方（小倉山、鳥居現存）に遷座し、その後、{{jalink|昭和|Q327567}}29年（{{jalink|1954年|Q18581}}）に現在地に鎮座していた{{jalink|天神信仰|Q66890725}}の{{jalink|菅原神社|Q48532247}}に周辺神社を集約して合祀し、二宮神社と改名した<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>。

二宮神社の名の由来は、祭神が箱根三姉妹の次女だからとも解釈できるが、公式には祭神が伴っていたと伝承される次男王子に由来すると説明されている<ref name="kanko"/>。

=== 祭神 ===
* 伊波乃比咩命（いわのひめのみこと） - {{jalink|事代主神}}（三島大明神）の后神の一柱。

『三宅記』の神話によると、三宅島の神社に祀られている三島大明神（事代主神）の三姉妹の后は、{{jalink|箱根|Q11603342}}{{jalink|芦ノ湖|Q187053}}周辺に住んでいた翁媼（老夫婦）の娘たちを、龍神（大蛇）から救って三宅島に迎えたものであり、長女が{{jalink|后神社 (三宅村)|Q134930558|后神社}}に祀られている伊賀牟比売命、次女が二宮神社に祀られている伊波乃比咩命、三女が{{jalink|御笏神社|Q110915859}}に祀られている佐伎多麻比咩命とされる<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html 三宅島の神社] - 延喜式神社の調査</ref>。

=== 脚注 ===

=== 外部リンク ===

'''二宮神社'''（にのみやじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|東京都|Q1490}}{{jalink|三宅村|Q1154797}}（{{jalink|三宅島|Q1075076}}）にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。

三宅島南東の港・坪田(つぼた)漁港の目前に鎮座している。

'''伊波乃比咩命神社'''（いわのひめのみことじんじゃ）を中心に{{jalink|式内社}}数社、その他周辺小社が数多く合祀されている<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - 三宅島観光協会</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - 延喜式神社の調査</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf 三宅島 p.84 (三宅島の式内社)] - 東京都/三宅島観光協会</ref>。

三宅島内にある{{jalink|事代主神}}（三島大明神）の后を祀る3つの神社の内の1つ<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html 歴史] - 東京都三宅島</ref>。

=== 歴史 ===
中心となっている伊波乃比咩命神社は、元は現在地の北方山中（現・伊波耶観音神社 (坪田観音)）にあり、その後、鎌倉初期に現在地の南西・大路池南方（小倉山、鳥居現存）に遷座し、その後、{{jalink|昭和|Q327567}}29年（{{jalink|1954年|Q18581}}）に現在地に鎮座していた{{jalink|天神信仰|Q66890725}}の{{jalink|菅原神社|Q48532247}}に周辺神社を集約して合祀し、二宮神社と改名した<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>。

二宮神社の名の由来は、祭神が箱根三姉妹の次女だからとも解釈できるが、公式には祭神が伴っていたと伝承される次男王子に由来すると説明されている<ref name="kanko"/>。

=== 祭神 ===
* 伊波乃比咩命（いわのひめのみこと） - {{jalink|事代主神}}（三島大明神）の后神の一柱。

『三宅記』の神話によると、三宅島の神社に祀られている三島大明神（事代主神）の三姉妹の后は、{{jalink|箱根|Q11603342}}{{jalink|芦ノ湖|Q187053}}周辺に住んでいた翁媼（老夫婦）の娘たちを、龍神（大蛇）から救って三宅島に迎えたものであり、長女が{{jalink|后神社 (三宅村)|Q134930558|后神社}}に祀られている伊賀牟比売命、次女が二宮神社に祀られている伊波乃比咩命、三女が{{jalink|御笏神社|Q110915859}}に祀られている佐伎多麻比咩命とされる<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html 三宅島の神社] - 延喜式神社の調査</ref>。

[[JA:二宮神社 (三宅村)]]

{{wikidata link|Q134930277}}

== Japanese content ==
{{神社
|名称   = 后神社
|画像= {{画像募集中|cat=東京都島嶼部}}
|所在地 = {{jalink|東京都|Q1490}}{{jalink|三宅村|Q1154797}}{{jalink|坪田村|Q11425774|坪田}}
|位置 = {{Coord|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E|display=inline,title}}
|祭神   = 伊波乃比咩命

|神事   = 
|地図 = {{Location map|Japan|label=二宮神社|coordinates=
{{coord2|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E}}}}
}}
'''二宮神社'''（にのみやじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|東京都|Q1490}}{{jalink|三宅村|Q1154797}}（{{jalink|三宅島|Q1075076}}）にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。

三宅島南東の港・坪田(つぼた)漁港の目前に鎮座している。

'''伊波乃比咩命神社'''（いわのひめのみことじんじゃ）を中心に{{jalink|式内社}}数社、その他周辺小社が数多く合祀されている<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - 三宅島観光協会</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - 延喜式神社の調査</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf 三宅島 p.84 (三宅島の式内社)] - 東京都/三宅島観光協会</ref>。

三宅島内にある{{jalink|事代主神}}（三島大明神）の后を祀る3つの神社の内の1つ<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html 歴史] - 東京都三宅島</ref>。

=== 歴史 ===
中心となっている伊波乃比咩命神社は、元は現在地の北方山中（現・伊波耶観音神社 (坪田観音)）にあり、その後、鎌倉初期に現在地の南西・大路池南方（小倉山、鳥居現存）に遷座し、その後、{{jalink|昭和|Q327567}}29年（{{jalink|1954年|Q18581}}）に現在地に鎮座していた{{jalink|天神信仰|Q66890725}}の{{jalink|菅原神社|Q48532247}}に周辺神社を集約して合祀し、二宮神社と改名した<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>。

二宮神社の名の由来は、祭神が箱根三姉妹の次女だからとも解釈できるが、公式には祭神が伴っていたと伝承される次男王子に由来すると説明されている<ref name="kanko"/>。

=== 祭神 ===
* 伊波乃比咩命（いわのひめのみこと） - {{jalink|事代主神}}（三島大明神）の后神の一柱。

『三宅記』の神話によると、三宅島の神社に祀られている三島大明神（事代主神）の三姉妹の后は、{{jalink|箱根|Q11603342}}{{jalink|芦ノ湖|Q187053}}周辺に住んでいた翁媼（老夫婦）の娘たちを、龍神（大蛇）から救って三宅島に迎えたものであり、長女が{{jalink|后神社 (三宅村)|Q134930558|后神社}}に祀られている伊賀牟比売命、次女が二宮神社に祀られている伊波乃比咩命、三女が{{jalink|御笏神社|Q110915859}}に祀られている佐伎多麻比咩命とされる<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html 三宅島の神社] - 延喜式神社の調査</ref>。

== Japanese content ==
{{神社
|名称   = 后神社

{{coord2|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E}}}}
}}
'''二宮神社'''（にのみやじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|東京都|Q1490}}{{jalink|三宅村|Q1154797}}（{{jalink|三宅島|Q1075076}}）にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。

三宅島南東の港・坪田(つぼた)漁港の目前に鎮座している。

'''伊波乃比咩命神社'''（いわのひめのみことじんじゃ）を中心に{{jalink|式内社}}数社、その他周辺小社が数多く合祀されている<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - 三宅島観光協会</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - 延喜式神社の調査</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf 三宅島 p.84 (三宅島の式内社)] - 東京都/三宅島観光協会</ref>。

三宅島内にある{{jalink|事代主神}}（三島大明神）の后を祀る3つの神社の内の1つ<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html 歴史] - 東京都三宅島</ref>。

=== 歴史 ===
中心となっている伊波乃比咩命神社は、元は現在地の北方山中（現・伊波耶観音神社 (坪田観音)）にあり、その後、鎌倉初期に現在地の南西・大路池南方（小倉山、鳥居現存）に遷座し、その後、{{jalink|昭和|Q327567}}29年（{{jalink|1954年|Q18581}}）に現在地に鎮座していた{{jalink|天神信仰|Q66890725}}の{{jalink|菅原神社|Q48532247}}に周辺神社を集約して合祀し、二宮神社と改名した<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>。

二宮神社の名の由来は、祭神が箱根三姉妹の次女だからとも解釈できるが、公式には祭神が伴っていたと伝承される次男王子に由来すると説明されている<ref name="kanko"/>。

=== 祭神 ===
* 伊波乃比咩命（いわのひめのみこと） - {{jalink|事代主神}}（三島大明神）の后神の一柱。

『三宅記』の神話によると、三宅島の神社に祀られている三島大明神（事代主神）の三姉妹の后は、{{jalink|箱根|Q11603342}}{{jalink|芦ノ湖|Q187053}}周辺に住んでいた翁媼（老夫婦）の娘たちを、龍神（大蛇）から救って三宅島に迎えたものであり、長女が{{jalink|后神社 (三宅村)|Q134930558|后神社}}に祀られている伊賀牟比売命、次女が二宮神社に祀られている伊波乃比咩命、三女が{{jalink|御笏神社|Q110915859}}に祀られている佐伎多麻比咩命とされる<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html 三宅島の神社] - 延喜式神社の調査</ref>。

'''二宮神社'''（にのみやじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|東京都|Q1490}}{{jalink|三宅村|Q1154797}}（{{jalink|三宅島|Q1075076}}）にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。

三宅島南東の港・坪田(つぼた)漁港の目前に鎮座している。

'''伊波乃比咩命神社'''（いわのひめのみことじんじゃ）を中心に{{jalink|式内社}}数社、その他周辺小社が数多く合祀されている<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - 三宅島観光協会</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - 延喜式神社の調査</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf 三宅島 p.84 (三宅島の式内社)] - 東京都/三宅島観光協会</ref>。

三宅島内にある{{jalink|事代主神}}（三島大明神）の后を祀る3つの神社の内の1つ<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html 歴史] - 東京都三宅島</ref>。

=== 歴史 ===
中心となっている伊波乃比咩命神社は、元は現在地の北方山中（現・伊波耶観音神社 (坪田観音)）にあり、その後、鎌倉初期に現在地の南西・大路池南方（小倉山、鳥居現存）に遷座し、その後、{{jalink|昭和|Q327567}}29年（{{jalink|1954年|Q18581}}）に現在地に鎮座していた{{jalink|天神信仰|Q66890725}}の{{jalink|菅原神社|Q48532247}}に周辺神社を集約して合祀し、二宮神社と改名した<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>。

二宮神社の名の由来は、祭神が箱根三姉妹の次女だからとも解釈できるが、公式には祭神が伴っていたと伝承される次男王子に由来すると説明されている<ref name="kanko"/>。

=== 祭神 ===
* 伊波乃比咩命（いわのひめのみこと） - {{jalink|事代主神}}（三島大明神）の后神の一柱。

『三宅記』の神話によると、三宅島の神社に祀られている三島大明神（事代主神）の三姉妹の后は、{{jalink|箱根|Q11603342}}{{jalink|芦ノ湖|Q187053}}周辺に住んでいた翁媼（老夫婦）の娘たちを、龍神（大蛇）から救って三宅島に迎えたものであり、長女が{{jalink|后神社 (三宅村)|Q134930558|后神社}}に祀られている伊賀牟比売命、次女が二宮神社に祀られている伊波乃比咩命、三女が{{jalink|御笏神社|Q110915859}}に祀られている佐伎多麻比咩命とされる<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html 三宅島の神社] - 延喜式神社の調査</ref>。

=== 脚注 ===

=== 外部リンク ===

'''二宮神社'''（にのみやじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|東京都|Q1490}}{{jalink|三宅村|Q1154797}}（{{jalink|三宅島|Q1075076}}）にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。

三宅島南東の港・坪田(つぼた)漁港の目前に鎮座している。

'''伊波乃比咩命神社'''（いわのひめのみことじんじゃ）を中心に{{jalink|式内社}}数社、その他周辺小社が数多く合祀されている<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - 三宅島観光協会</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - 延喜式神社の調査</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf 三宅島 p.84 (三宅島の式内社)] - 東京都/三宅島観光協会</ref>。

三宅島内にある{{jalink|事代主神}}（三島大明神）の后を祀る3つの神社の内の1つ<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html 歴史] - 東京都三宅島</ref>。

=== 歴史 ===
中心となっている伊波乃比咩命神社は、元は現在地の北方山中（現・伊波耶観音神社 (坪田観音)）にあり、その後、鎌倉初期に現在地の南西・大路池南方（小倉山、鳥居現存）に遷座し、その後、{{jalink|昭和|Q327567}}29年（{{jalink|1954年|Q18581}}）に現在地に鎮座していた{{jalink|天神信仰|Q66890725}}の{{jalink|菅原神社|Q48532247}}に周辺神社を集約して合祀し、二宮神社と改名した<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>。

二宮神社の名の由来は、祭神が箱根三姉妹の次女だからとも解釈できるが、公式には祭神が伴っていたと伝承される次男王子に由来すると説明されている<ref name="kanko"/>。

=== 祭神 ===
* 伊波乃比咩命（いわのひめのみこと） - {{jalink|事代主神}}（三島大明神）の后神の一柱。

『三宅記』の神話によると、三宅島の神社に祀られている三島大明神（事代主神）の三姉妹の后は、{{jalink|箱根|Q11603342}}{{jalink|芦ノ湖|Q187053}}周辺に住んでいた翁媼（老夫婦）の娘たちを、龍神（大蛇）から救って三宅島に迎えたものであり、長女が{{jalink|后神社 (三宅村)|Q134930558|后神社}}に祀られている伊賀牟比売命、次女が二宮神社に祀られている伊波乃比咩命、三女が{{jalink|御笏神社|Q110915859}}に祀られている佐伎多麻比咩命とされる<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html 三宅島の神社] - 延喜式神社の調査</ref>。

== Japanese content ==
{{神社
|名称   = 后神社
|画像= {{画像募集中|cat=東京都島嶼部}}

{{coord2|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E}}}}
}}
'''二宮神社'''（にのみやじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|東京都|Q1490}}{{jalink|三宅村|Q1154797}}（{{jalink|三宅島|Q1075076}}）にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。

三宅島南東の港・坪田(つぼた)漁港の目前に鎮座している。

'''伊波乃比咩命神社'''（いわのひめのみことじんじゃ）を中心に{{jalink|式内社}}数社、その他周辺小社が数多く合祀されている<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - 三宅島観光協会</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - 延喜式神社の調査</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf 三宅島 p.84 (三宅島の式内社)] - 東京都/三宅島観光協会</ref>。

三宅島内にある{{jalink|事代主神}}（三島大明神）の后を祀る3つの神社の内の1つ<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html 歴史] - 東京都三宅島</ref>。

=== 歴史 ===
中心となっている伊波乃比咩命神社は、元は現在地の北方山中（現・伊波耶観音神社 (坪田観音)）にあり、その後、鎌倉初期に現在地の南西・大路池南方（小倉山、鳥居現存）に遷座し、その後、{{jalink|昭和|Q327567}}29年（{{jalink|1954年|Q18581}}）に現在地に鎮座していた{{jalink|天神信仰|Q66890725}}の{{jalink|菅原神社|Q48532247}}に周辺神社を集約して合祀し、二宮神社と改名した<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>。

二宮神社の名の由来は、祭神が箱根三姉妹の次女だからとも解釈できるが、公式には祭神が伴っていたと伝承される次男王子に由来すると説明されている<ref name="kanko"/>。

=== 祭神 ===
* 伊波乃比咩命（いわのひめのみこと） - {{jalink|事代主神}}（三島大明神）の后神の一柱。

『三宅記』の神話によると、三宅島の神社に祀られている三島大明神（事代主神）の三姉妹の后は、{{jalink|箱根|Q11603342}}{{jalink|芦ノ湖|Q187053}}周辺に住んでいた翁媼（老夫婦）の娘たちを、龍神（大蛇）から救って三宅島に迎えたものであり、長女が{{jalink|后神社 (三宅村)|Q134930558|后神社}}に祀られている伊賀牟比売命、次女が二宮神社に祀られている伊波乃比咩命、三女が{{jalink|御笏神社|Q110915859}}に祀られている佐伎多麻比咩命とされる<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html 三宅島の神社] - 延喜式神社の調査</ref>。

'''二宮神社'''（にのみやじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|東京都|Q1490}}{{jalink|三宅村|Q1154797}}（{{jalink|三宅島|Q1075076}}）にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。

三宅島南東の港・坪田(つぼた)漁港の目前に鎮座している。

'''伊波乃比咩命神社'''（いわのひめのみことじんじゃ）を中心に{{jalink|式内社}}数社、その他周辺小社が数多く合祀されている<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - 三宅島観光協会</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - 延喜式神社の調査</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf 三宅島 p.84 (三宅島の式内社)] - 東京都/三宅島観光協会</ref>。

三宅島内にある{{jalink|事代主神}}（三島大明神）の后を祀る3つの神社の内の1つ<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html 歴史] - 東京都三宅島</ref>。

=== 歴史 ===
中心となっている伊波乃比咩命神社は、元は現在地の北方山中（現・伊波耶観音神社 (坪田観音)）にあり、その後、鎌倉初期に現在地の南西・大路池南方（小倉山、鳥居現存）に遷座し、その後、{{jalink|昭和|Q327567}}29年（{{jalink|1954年|Q18581}}）に現在地に鎮座していた{{jalink|天神信仰|Q66890725}}の{{jalink|菅原神社|Q48532247}}に周辺神社を集約して合祀し、二宮神社と改名した<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>。

二宮神社の名の由来は、祭神が箱根三姉妹の次女だからとも解釈できるが、公式には祭神が伴っていたと伝承される次男王子に由来すると説明されている<ref name="kanko"/>。

=== 祭神 ===
* 伊波乃比咩命（いわのひめのみこと） - {{jalink|事代主神}}（三島大明神）の后神の一柱。

『三宅記』の神話によると、三宅島の神社に祀られている三島大明神（事代主神）の三姉妹の后は、{{jalink|箱根|Q11603342}}{{jalink|芦ノ湖|Q187053}}周辺に住んでいた翁媼（老夫婦）の娘たちを、龍神（大蛇）から救って三宅島に迎えたものであり、長女が{{jalink|后神社 (三宅村)|Q134930558|后神社}}に祀られている伊賀牟比売命、次女が二宮神社に祀られている伊波乃比咩命、三女が{{jalink|御笏神社|Q110915859}}に祀られている佐伎多麻比咩命とされる<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html 三宅島の神社] - 延喜式神社の調査</ref>。

== Japanese content ==
{{神社
|名称   = Kisaki Shrine
|画像= {{画像募集中|cat=Tokyo Islands}}
|所在地 = {{jalink|Tokyo|Q1490}}{{jalink|Miyake Village|Q1154797}}{{jalink|Tsubota Village|Q11425774|Tsubota}}
|位置 = {{Coord|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E|display=inline,title}}
|祭神   = Iwanohime-no-Mikoto

{{coord2|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E}}}}
}}
'''Ninomiya Shrine''' (にのみやじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Miyake Village|Q1154797}} ({{jalink|Miyake-jima|Q1075076}}), {{jalink|Tokyo|Q1490}}.

It stands directly in front of Tsubota fishing port in the southeast of Miyake-jima.

Several {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} and many nearby small shrines are merged here, centered on '''Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' (いわのひめのみことじんじゃ)<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima p.84 (Shikinaisha of Miyakejima)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref>.

It is one of three shrines on Miyake-jima that enshrine the consort of {{jalink|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} (Mishima Daimyōjin)<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html History] - Miyakejima, Tokyo</ref>.

=== History ===
The central Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine originally stood in the northern mountains of the current site (present Iwaha Kannon Shrine / Tsubota Kannon), then in the early Kamakura period moved to southwest of the current site, south of Ōji-ike (Ogura-yama, with torii still extant). Later, in {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 29 ({{jalink|1954|Q18581}}), nearby shrines were consolidated into and merged with {{jalink|Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247}} (of {{jalink|Tenjin worship|Q66890725}}), which stood at the current site, and it was renamed Ninomiya Shrine<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>.

The name Ninomiya Shrine could be interpreted as deriving from the deity being the second daughter among the Hakone three sisters, but the official explanation says it derives from the second prince who is traditionally said to have accompanied the deity<ref name="kanko"/>.

=== Enshrined deity ===
* Iwanohime-no-Mikoto (いわのひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{jalink|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} (Mishima Daimyōjin).

According to the mythology in ''Miyakeki'', the three sister-consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), enshrined in shrines on Miyake-jima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{jalink|Lake Ashi|Q187053}} in {{jalink|Hakone|Q11603342}}, rescued from a dragon deity (great serpent) and brought to Miyake-jima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{jalink|Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto enshrined at Ninomiya Shrine, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{jalink|Mishaku Shrine|Q110915859}}<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref>.

'''Ninomiya Shrine''' (にのみやじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Miyake Village|Q1154797}} ({{jalink|Miyake-jima|Q1075076}}), {{jalink|Tokyo|Q1490}}.

It stands directly in front of Tsubota fishing port in the southeast of Miyake-jima.

Several {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} and many nearby small shrines are merged here, centered on '''Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' (いわのひめのみことじんじゃ)<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima p.84 (Shikinaisha of Miyakejima)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref>.

It is one of three shrines on Miyake-jima that enshrine the consort of {{jalink|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} (Mishima Daimyōjin)<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html History] - Miyakejima, Tokyo</ref>.

=== History ===
The central Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine originally stood in the northern mountains of the current site (present Iwaha Kannon Shrine / Tsubota Kannon), then in the early Kamakura period moved to southwest of the current site, south of Ōji-ike (Ogura-yama, with torii still extant). Later, in {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 29 ({{jalink|1954|Q18581}}), nearby shrines were consolidated into and merged with {{jalink|Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247}} (of {{jalink|Tenjin worship|Q66890725}}), which stood at the current site, and it was renamed Ninomiya Shrine<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>.

The name Ninomiya Shrine could be interpreted as deriving from the deity being the second daughter among the Hakone three sisters, but the official explanation says it derives from the second prince who is traditionally said to have accompanied the deity<ref name="kanko"/>.

=== Enshrined deity ===
* Iwanohime-no-Mikoto (いわのひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{jalink|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} (Mishima Daimyōjin).

According to the mythology in ''Miyakeki'', the three sister-consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), enshrined in shrines on Miyake-jima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{jalink|Lake Ashi|Q187053}} in {{jalink|Hakone|Q11603342}}, rescued from a dragon deity (great serpent) and brought to Miyake-jima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{jalink|Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto enshrined at Ninomiya Shrine, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{jalink|Mishaku Shrine|Q110915859}}<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref>.

=== Notes ===

=== External links ===

The central Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine originally stood in the northern mountains of the current site (present Iwaha Kannon Shrine / Tsubota Kannon), then in the early Kamakura period moved to southwest of the current site, south of Ōji-ike (Ogura-yama, with torii still extant). The name Ninomiya Shrine could be interpreted as deriving from the deity being the second daughter among the Hakone three sisters, but the official explanation says it derives from the second prince who is traditionally said to have accompanied the deity<ref name="kanko"/>.

{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E}}}}
}}
'''Ninomiya Shrine''' (にのみやじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Miyake Village|Q1154797}} ({{jalink|Miyake-jima|Q1075076}}), {{jalink|Tokyo|Q1490}}.

It stands directly in front of Tsubota fishing port in the southeast of Miyake-jima.

Several {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} and many nearby small shrines are merged here, centered on '''Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' (いわのひめのみことじんじゃ)<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima p.84 (Shikinaisha of Miyakejima)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref>.

It is one of three shrines on Miyake-jima that enshrine the consort of {{jalink|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} (Mishima Daimyōjin)<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html History] - Miyakejima, Tokyo</ref>.

=== History ===
The central Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine originally stood in the northern mountains of the current site (present Iwaha Kannon Shrine / Tsubota Kannon), then in the early Kamakura period moved to southwest of the current site, south of Ōji-ike (Ogura-yama, with torii still extant). Later, in {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 29 ({{jalink|1954|Q18581}}), nearby shrines were consolidated into and merged with {{jalink|Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247}} (of {{jalink|Tenjin worship|Q66890725}}), which stood at the current site, and it was renamed Ninomiya Shrine<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>.

The name Ninomiya Shrine could be interpreted as deriving from the deity being the second daughter among the Hakone three sisters, but the official explanation says it derives from the second prince who is traditionally said to have accompanied the deity<ref name="kanko"/>.

=== Enshrined deity ===
* Iwanohime-no-Mikoto (いわのひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{jalink|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} (Mishima Daimyōjin).

According to the mythology in ''Miyakeki'', the three sister-consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), enshrined in shrines on Miyake-jima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{jalink|Lake Ashi|Q187053}} in {{jalink|Hakone|Q11603342}}, rescued from a dragon deity (great serpent) and brought to Miyake-jima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{jalink|Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto enshrined at Ninomiya Shrine, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{jalink|Mishaku Shrine|Q110915859}}<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref>.

'''Ninomiya Shrine''' (にのみやじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Miyake Village|Q1154797}} ({{jalink|Miyake-jima|Q1075076}}), {{jalink|Tokyo|Q1490}}.

It stands directly in front of Tsubota fishing port in the southeast of Miyake-jima.

Several {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} and many nearby small shrines are merged here, centered on '''Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' (いわのひめのみことじんじゃ)<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima p.84 (Shikinaisha of Miyakejima)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref>.

It is one of three shrines on Miyake-jima that enshrine the consort of {{jalink|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} (Mishima Daimyōjin)<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html History] - Miyakejima, Tokyo</ref>.

=== History ===
The central Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine originally stood in the northern mountains of the current site (present Iwaha Kannon Shrine / Tsubota Kannon), then in the early Kamakura period moved to southwest of the current site, south of Ōji-ike (Ogura-yama, with torii still extant). Later, in {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 29 ({{jalink|1954|Q18581}}), nearby shrines were consolidated into and merged with {{jalink|Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247}} (of {{jalink|Tenjin worship|Q66890725}}), which stood at the current site, and it was renamed Ninomiya Shrine<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>.

The name Ninomiya Shrine could be interpreted as deriving from the deity being the second daughter among the Hakone three sisters, but the official explanation says it derives from the second prince who is traditionally said to have accompanied the deity<ref name="kanko"/>.

=== Enshrined deity ===
* Iwanohime-no-Mikoto (いわのひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{jalink|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} (Mishima Daimyōjin).

According to the mythology in ''Miyakeki'', the three sister-consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), enshrined in shrines on Miyake-jima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{jalink|Lake Ashi|Q187053}} in {{jalink|Hakone|Q11603342}}, rescued from a dragon deity (great serpent) and brought to Miyake-jima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{jalink|Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto enshrined at Ninomiya Shrine, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{jalink|Mishaku Shrine|Q110915859}}<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref>.

{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E}}}}
}}
'''Ninomiya Shrine''' (にのみやじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Miyake Village|Q1154797}} ({{jalink|Miyake-jima|Q1075076}}), {{jalink|Tokyo|Q1490}}.

It stands directly in front of Tsubota fishing port in the southeast of Miyake-jima.

Several {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} and many nearby small shrines are merged here, centered on '''Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' (いわのひめのみことじんじゃ)<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima p.84 (Shikinaisha of Miyakejima)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref>.

It is one of three shrines on Miyake-jima that enshrine the consort of {{jalink|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} (Mishima Daimyōjin)<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html History] - Miyakejima, Tokyo</ref>.

=== History ===
The central Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine originally stood in the northern mountains of the current site (present Iwaha Kannon Shrine / Tsubota Kannon), then in the early Kamakura period moved to southwest of the current site, south of Ōji-ike (Ogura-yama, with torii still extant). Later, in {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 29 ({{jalink|1954|Q18581}}), nearby shrines were consolidated into and merged with {{jalink|Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247}} (of {{jalink|Tenjin worship|Q66890725}}), which stood at the current site, and it was renamed Ninomiya Shrine<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>.

The name Ninomiya Shrine could be interpreted as deriving from the deity being the second daughter among the Hakone three sisters, but the official explanation says it derives from the second prince who is traditionally said to have accompanied the deity<ref name="kanko"/>.

=== Enshrined deity ===
* Iwanohime-no-Mikoto (いわのひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{jalink|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} (Mishima Daimyōjin).

According to the mythology in ''Miyakeki'', the three sister-consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), enshrined in shrines on Miyake-jima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{jalink|Lake Ashi|Q187053}} in {{jalink|Hakone|Q11603342}}, rescued from a dragon deity (great serpent) and brought to Miyake-jima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{jalink|Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto enshrined at Ninomiya Shrine, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{jalink|Mishaku Shrine|Q110915859}}<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref>.

'''Ninomiya Shrine''' (にのみやじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Miyake Village|Q1154797}} ({{jalink|Miyake-jima|Q1075076}}), {{jalink|Tokyo|Q1490}}.

It stands directly in front of Tsubota fishing port in the southeast of Miyake-jima.

Several {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} and many nearby small shrines are merged here, centered on '''Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' (いわのひめのみことじんじゃ)<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima p.84 (Shikinaisha of Miyakejima)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref>.

It is one of three shrines on Miyake-jima that enshrine the consort of {{jalink|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} (Mishima Daimyōjin)<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html History] - Miyakejima, Tokyo</ref>.

=== History ===
The central Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine originally stood in the northern mountains of the current site (present Iwaha Kannon Shrine / Tsubota Kannon), then in the early Kamakura period moved to southwest of the current site, south of Ōji-ike (Ogura-yama, with torii still extant). Later, in {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 29 ({{jalink|1954|Q18581}}), nearby shrines were consolidated into and merged with {{jalink|Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247}} (of {{jalink|Tenjin worship|Q66890725}}), which stood at the current site, and it was renamed Ninomiya Shrine<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>.

The name Ninomiya Shrine could be interpreted as deriving from the deity being the second daughter among the Hakone three sisters, but the official explanation says it derives from the second prince who is traditionally said to have accompanied the deity<ref name="kanko"/>.

=== Enshrined deity ===
* Iwanohime-no-Mikoto (いわのひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{jalink|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} (Mishima Daimyōjin).

According to the mythology in ''Miyakeki'', the three sister-consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), enshrined in shrines on Miyake-jima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{jalink|Lake Ashi|Q187053}} in {{jalink|Hakone|Q11603342}}, rescued from a dragon deity (great serpent) and brought to Miyake-jima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{jalink|Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto enshrined at Ninomiya Shrine, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{jalink|Mishaku Shrine|Q110915859}}<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref>.

'''Ninomiya Shrine''' (にのみやじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Miyake Village|Q1154797}} ({{jalink|Miyake-jima|Q1075076}}), {{jalink|Tokyo|Q1490}}.

It stands directly in front of Tsubota fishing port in the southeast of Miyake-jima.

Several {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} and many nearby small shrines are merged here, centered on '''Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' (いわのひめのみことじんじゃ)<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima p.84 (Shikinaisha of Miyakejima)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref>.

It is one of three shrines on Miyake-jima that enshrine the consort of {{jalink|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} (Mishima Daimyōjin)<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html History] - Miyakejima, Tokyo</ref>.

=== History ===
The central Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine originally stood in the northern mountains of the current site (present Iwaha Kannon Shrine / Tsubota Kannon), then in the early Kamakura period moved to southwest of the current site, south of Ōji-ike (Ogura-yama, with torii still extant). Later, in {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 29 ({{jalink|1954|Q18581}}), nearby shrines were consolidated into and merged with {{jalink|Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247}} (of {{jalink|Tenjin worship|Q66890725}}), which stood at the current site, and it was renamed Ninomiya Shrine<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>.

The name Ninomiya Shrine could be interpreted as deriving from the deity being the second daughter among the Hakone three sisters, but the official explanation says it derives from the second prince who is traditionally said to have accompanied the deity<ref name="kanko"/>.

=== Enshrined deity ===
* Iwanohime-no-Mikoto (いわのひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{jalink|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} (Mishima Daimyōjin).

According to the mythology in ''Miyakeki'', the three sister-consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), enshrined in shrines on Miyake-jima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{jalink|Lake Ashi|Q187053}} in {{jalink|Hakone|Q11603342}}, rescued from a dragon deity (great serpent) and brought to Miyake-jima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{jalink|Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto enshrined at Ninomiya Shrine, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{jalink|Mishaku Shrine|Q110915859}}<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref>.

{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E}}}}
}}
'''Ninomiya Shrine''' (にのみやじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Miyake Village|Q1154797}} ({{jalink|Miyake-jima|Q1075076}}), {{jalink|Tokyo|Q1490}}.

It stands directly in front of Tsubota fishing port in the southeast of Miyake-jima.

Several {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} and many nearby small shrines are merged here, centered on '''Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' (いわのひめのみことじんじゃ)<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima p.84 (Shikinaisha of Miyakejima)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref>.

It is one of three shrines on Miyake-jima that enshrine the consort of {{jalink|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} (Mishima Daimyōjin)<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html History] - Miyakejima, Tokyo</ref>.

=== History ===
The central Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine originally stood in the northern mountains of the current site (present Iwaha Kannon Shrine / Tsubota Kannon), then in the early Kamakura period moved to southwest of the current site, south of Ōji-ike (Ogura-yama, with torii still extant). Later, in {{jalink|Shōwa|Q327567}} 29 ({{jalink|1954|Q18581}}), nearby shrines were consolidated into and merged with {{jalink|Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247}} (of {{jalink|Tenjin worship|Q66890725}}), which stood at the current site, and it was renamed Ninomiya Shrine<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>.

The name Ninomiya Shrine could be interpreted as deriving from the deity being the second daughter among the Hakone three sisters, but the official explanation says it derives from the second prince who is traditionally said to have accompanied the deity<ref name="kanko"/>.

=== Enshrined deity ===
* Iwanohime-no-Mikoto (いわのひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{jalink|Kotoshironushi-no-Kami}} (Mishima Daimyōjin).

According to the mythology in ''Miyakeki'', the three sister-consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), enshrined in shrines on Miyake-jima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{jalink|Lake Ashi|Q187053}} in {{jalink|Hakone|Q11603342}}, rescued from a dragon deity (great serpent) and brought to Miyake-jima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{jalink|Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto enshrined at Ninomiya Shrine, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{jalink|Mishaku Shrine|Q110915859}}<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref>.

It stands directly in front of Tsubota fishing port in the southeast of Miyake-jima.

Several {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} and many nearby small shrines are merged here, centered on '''Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' (いわのひめのみことじんじゃ)<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima p.84 (Shikinaisha of Miyakejima)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref>.

It is one of three shrines on Miyake-jima that enshrine the consort of {{ill||lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}} (Mishima Daimyōjin)<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html History] - Miyakejima, Tokyo</ref>.

=== History ===
The central Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine originally stood in the northern mountains of the current site (present Iwaha Kannon Shrine / Tsubota Kannon), then in the early Kamakura period moved to southwest of the current site, south of Ōji-ike (Ogura-yama, with torii still extant). Later, in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 29 ({{ill|lt=1954|Q18581|WD=Q18581}}), nearby shrines were consolidated into and merged with {{ill|lt=Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247|WD=Q48532247}} (of {{ill|lt=Tenjin worship|Q66890725|WD=Q66890725}}), which stood at the current site, and it was renamed Ninomiya Shrine<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>.

The name Ninomiya Shrine could be interpreted as deriving from the deity being the second daughter among the Hakone three sisters, but the official explanation says it derives from the second prince who is traditionally said to have accompanied the deity<ref name="kanko"/>.

=== Enshrined deity ===
* Iwanohime-no-Mikoto (いわのひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{ill||lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}} (Mishima Daimyōjin).

According to the mythology in ''Miyakeki'', the three sister-consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), enshrined in shrines on Miyake-jima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{ill|lt=Lake Ashi|Q187053|WD=Q187053}} in {{ill|lt=Hakone|Q11603342|WD=Q11603342}}, rescued from a dragon deity (great serpent) and brought to Miyake-jima. {{coord2|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E}}}}
}}
'''Ninomiya Shrine''' (にのみやじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797}} ({{ill|lt=Miyake-jima|Q1075076|WD=Q1075076}}), {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490}}.

It stands directly in front of Tsubota fishing port in the southeast of Miyake-jima.

Several {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} and many nearby small shrines are merged here, centered on '''Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' (いわのひめのみことじんじゃ)<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima p.84 (Shikinaisha of Miyakejima)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref>.

It is one of three shrines on Miyake-jima that enshrine the consort of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}} (Mishima Daimyōjin)<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html History] - Miyakejima, Tokyo</ref>.

=== History ===
The central Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine originally stood in the northern mountains of the current site (present Iwaha Kannon Shrine / Tsubota Kannon), then in the early Kamakura period moved to southwest of the current site, south of Ōji-ike (Ogura-yama, with torii still extant). Later, in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 29 ({{ill|lt=1954|Q18581|WD=Q18581}}), nearby shrines were consolidated into and merged with {{ill|lt=Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247|WD=Q48532247}} (of {{ill|lt=Tenjin worship|Q66890725|WD=Q66890725}}), which stood at the current site, and it was renamed Ninomiya Shrine<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>.

The name Ninomiya Shrine could be interpreted as deriving from the deity being the second daughter among the Hakone three sisters, but the official explanation says it derives from the second prince who is traditionally said to have accompanied the deity<ref name="kanko"/>.

=== Enshrined deity ===
* Iwanohime-no-Mikoto (いわのひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}} (Mishima Daimyōjin).

According to the mythology in ''Miyakeki'', the three sister-consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), enshrined in shrines on Miyake-jima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{ill|lt=Lake Ashi|Q187053|WD=Q187053}} in {{ill|lt=Hakone|Q11603342|WD=Q11603342}}, rescued from a dragon deity (great serpent) and brought to Miyake-jima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{ill|lt=Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|WD=Q134930558|lt=Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto enshrined at Ninomiya Shrine, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{ill|lt=Mishaku Shrine|Q110915859|WD=Q110915859}}<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref>.

* Iwanohime-no-Mikoto (いわのひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (Mishima Daimyōjin).

According to the mythology in ''Miyakeki'', the three sister-consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), enshrined in shrines on Miyake-jima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{ill|lt=Lake Ashi|Q187053|WD=Q187053}} in {{ill|lt=Hakone|Q11603342|WD=Q11603342}}, rescued from a dragon deity (great serpent) and brought to Miyake-jima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{ill|lt=Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|WD=Q134930558|lt=Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto enshrined at Ninomiya Shrine, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{ill|lt=Mishaku Shrine|Q110915859|WD=Q110915859}}<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref>.

== Japanese content ==
{{神社
|名称   = Kisaki Shrine
|画像= {{画像募集中|cat=Tokyo Islands}}
|所在地 = {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490}}{{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797}}{{ill|lt=Tsubota Village|Q11425774|WD=Q11425774|lt=Tsubota}}

{{coord2|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E}}}}
}}
'''Ninomiya Shrine''' (にのみやじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797}} ({{ill|lt=Miyake-jima|Q1075076|WD=Q1075076}}), {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490}}.

It stands directly in front of Tsubota fishing port in the southeast of Miyake-jima.

Several {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} and many nearby small shrines are merged here, centered on '''Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' (いわのひめのみことじんじゃ)<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima p.84 (Shikinaisha of Miyakejima)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref>.

It is one of three shrines on Miyake-jima that enshrine the consort of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (Mishima Daimyōjin)<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html History] - Miyakejima, Tokyo</ref>.

=== History ===
The central Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine originally stood in the northern mountains of the current site (present Iwaha Kannon Shrine / Tsubota Kannon), then in the early Kamakura period moved to southwest of the current site, south of Ōji-ike (Ogura-yama, with torii still extant). Later, in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 29 ({{ill|lt=1954|Q18581|WD=Q18581}}), nearby shrines were consolidated into and merged with {{ill|lt=Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247|WD=Q48532247}} (of {{ill|lt=Tenjin worship|Q66890725|WD=Q66890725}}), which stood at the current site, and it was renamed Ninomiya Shrine<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>.

The name Ninomiya Shrine could be interpreted as deriving from the deity being the second daughter among the Hakone three sisters, but the official explanation says it derives from the second prince who is traditionally said to have accompanied the deity<ref name="kanko"/>.

=== Enshrined deity ===
* Iwanohime-no-Mikoto (いわのひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (Mishima Daimyōjin).

According to the mythology in ''Miyakeki'', the three sister-consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), enshrined in shrines on Miyake-jima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{ill|lt=Lake Ashi|Q187053|WD=Q187053}} in {{ill|lt=Hakone|Q11603342|WD=Q11603342}}, rescued from a dragon deity (great serpent) and brought to Miyake-jima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{ill|lt=Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|WD=Q134930558|lt=Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto enshrined at Ninomiya Shrine, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{ill|lt=Mishaku Shrine|Q110915859|WD=Q110915859}}<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref>.

{{coord2|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E}}}}
}}
'''Ninomiya Shrine''' (にのみやじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797}} ({{ill|lt=Miyake-jima|Q1075076|WD=Q1075076}}), {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490}}.

It stands directly in front of Tsubota fishing port in the southeast of Miyake-jima.

Several {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} and many nearby small shrines are merged here, centered on '''Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' (いわのひめのみことじんじゃ)<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima p.84 (Shikinaisha of Miyakejima)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref>.

It is one of three shrines on Miyake-jima that enshrine the consort of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (Mishima Daimyōjin)<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html History] - Miyakejima, Tokyo</ref>.

=== History ===
The central Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine originally stood in the northern mountains of the current site (present Iwaha Kannon Shrine / Tsubota Kannon), then in the early Kamakura period moved to southwest of the current site, south of Ōji-ike (Ogura-yama, with torii still extant). Later, in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 29 ({{ill|lt=1954|Q18581|WD=Q18581}}), nearby shrines were consolidated into and merged with {{ill|lt=Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247|WD=Q48532247}} (of {{ill|lt=Tenjin worship|Q66890725|WD=Q66890725}}), which stood at the current site, and it was renamed Ninomiya Shrine<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>.

The name Ninomiya Shrine could be interpreted as deriving from the deity being the second daughter among the Hakone three sisters, but the official explanation says it derives from the second prince who is traditionally said to have accompanied the deity<ref name="kanko"/>.

=== Enshrined deity ===
* Iwanohime-no-Mikoto (いわのひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (Mishima Daimyōjin).

According to the mythology in ''Miyakeki'', the three sister-consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), enshrined in shrines on Miyake-jima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{ill|lt=Lake Ashi|Q187053|WD=Q187053}} in {{ill|lt=Hakone|Q11603342|WD=Q11603342}}, rescued from a dragon deity (great serpent) and brought to Miyake-jima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{ill|lt=Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|WD=Q134930558|lt=Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto enshrined at Ninomiya Shrine, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{ill|lt=Mishaku Shrine|Q110915859|WD=Q110915859}}<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref>.

'''Ninomiya Shrine''' (にのみやじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797}} ({{ill|lt=Miyake-jima|Q1075076|WD=Q1075076}}), {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490}}.

It stands directly in front of Tsubota fishing port in the southeast of Miyake-jima.

Several {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} and many nearby small shrines are merged here, centered on '''Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' (いわのひめのみことじんじゃ)<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima p.84 (Shikinaisha of Miyakejima)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref>.

It is one of three shrines on Miyake-jima that enshrine the consort of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (Mishima Daimyōjin)<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html History] - Miyakejima, Tokyo</ref>.

=== History ===
The central Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine originally stood in the northern mountains of the current site (present Iwaha Kannon Shrine / Tsubota Kannon), then in the early Kamakura period moved to southwest of the current site, south of Ōji-ike (Ogura-yama, with torii still extant). Later, in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567}} 29 ({{ill|lt=1954|Q18581|WD=Q18581}}), nearby shrines were consolidated into and merged with {{ill|lt=Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247|WD=Q48532247}} (of {{ill|lt=Tenjin worship|Q66890725|WD=Q66890725}}), which stood at the current site, and it was renamed Ninomiya Shrine<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>.

The name Ninomiya Shrine could be interpreted as deriving from the deity being the second daughter among the Hakone three sisters, but the official explanation says it derives from the second prince who is traditionally said to have accompanied the deity<ref name="kanko"/>.

=== Enshrined deity ===
* Iwanohime-no-Mikoto (いわのひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}} (Mishima Daimyōjin).

According to the mythology in ''Miyakeki'', the three sister-consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), enshrined in shrines on Miyake-jima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{ill|lt=Lake Ashi|Q187053|WD=Q187053}} in {{ill|lt=Hakone|Q11603342|WD=Q11603342}}, rescued from a dragon deity (great serpent) and brought to Miyake-jima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{ill|lt=Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|WD=Q134930558|lt=Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto enshrined at Ninomiya Shrine, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{ill|lt=Mishaku Shrine|Q110915859|WD=Q110915859}}<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref>.

== Japanese content ==
{{神社
|名称   = Kisaki Shrine
|画像= {{画像募集中|cat=Tokyo Islands}}
|所在地 = {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}{{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797|1=Miyake, Tokyo}}{{ill|lt=Tsubota Village|Q11425774|WD=Q11425774|lt=Tsubota|1=Tsubota}}
|位置 = {{Coord|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E|display=inline,title}}
|祭神   = Iwanohime-no-Mikoto
|社格   = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}

{{coord2|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E}}}}
}}
'''Ninomiya Shrine''' (にのみやじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797|1=Miyake, Tokyo}} ({{ill|lt=Miyake-jima|Q1075076|WD=Q1075076|1=Miyake-jima}}), {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}.

It stands directly in front of Tsubota fishing port in the southeast of Miyake-jima.

Several {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} and many nearby small shrines are merged here, centered on '''Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' (いわのひめのみことじんじゃ)<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima p.84 (Shikinaisha of Miyakejima)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref>.

It is one of three shrines on Miyake-jima that enshrine the consort of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} (Mishima Daimyōjin)<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html History] - Miyakejima, Tokyo</ref>.

=== History ===
The central Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine originally stood in the northern mountains of the current site (present Iwaha Kannon Shrine / Tsubota Kannon), then in the early Kamakura period moved to southwest of the current site, south of Ōji-ike (Ogura-yama, with torii still extant). Later, in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 29 ({{ill|lt=1954|Q18581|WD=Q18581|1=1954}}), nearby shrines were consolidated into and merged with {{ill|lt=Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247|WD=Q48532247|1=Sugawara Shrine (disambiguation)}} (of {{ill|lt=Tenjin worship|Q66890725|WD=Q66890725|1=Tenjin faith}}), which stood at the current site, and it was renamed Ninomiya Shrine<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>.

The name Ninomiya Shrine could be interpreted as deriving from the deity being the second daughter among the Hakone three sisters, but the official explanation says it derives from the second prince who is traditionally said to have accompanied the deity<ref name="kanko"/>.

=== Enshrined deity ===
* Iwanohime-no-Mikoto (いわのひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} (Mishima Daimyōjin).

According to the mythology in ''Miyakeki'', the three sister-consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), enshrined in shrines on Miyake-jima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{ill|lt=Lake Ashi|Q187053|WD=Q187053|1=Lake Ashi}} in {{ill|lt=Hakone|Q11603342|WD=Q11603342|1=Hakone}}, rescued from a dragon deity (great serpent) and brought to Miyake-jima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{ill|lt=Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|WD=Q134930558|lt=Kisaki Shrine|1=Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto enshrined at Ninomiya Shrine, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{ill|lt=Mishaku Shrine|Q110915859|WD=Q110915859|1=Oshaku Shrine}}<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref>.

{{nihongo|'''Ninomiya Shrine'''|二宮神社 (三宅村)}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Izu Province]].

'''Ninomiya Shrine''' (にのみやじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797|1=Miyake, Tokyo}} ({{ill|lt=Miyake-jima|Q1075076|WD=Q1075076|1=Miyake-jima}}), {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}.

It stands directly in front of Tsubota fishing port in the southeast of Miyake-jima.

Several {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} and many nearby small shrines are merged here, centered on '''Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' (いわのひめのみことじんじゃ)<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima p.84 (Shikinaisha of Miyakejima)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref>.

It is one of three shrines on Miyake-jima that enshrine the consort of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} (Mishima Daimyōjin)<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html History] - Miyakejima, Tokyo</ref>.

=== History ===
The central Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine originally stood in the northern mountains of the current site (present Iwaha Kannon Shrine / Tsubota Kannon), then in the early Kamakura period moved to southwest of the current site, south of Ōji-ike (Ogura-yama, with torii still extant). Later, in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 29 ({{ill|lt=1954|Q18581|WD=Q18581|1=1954}}), nearby shrines were consolidated into and merged with {{ill|lt=Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247|WD=Q48532247|1=Sugawara Shrine (disambiguation)}} (of {{ill|lt=Tenjin worship|Q66890725|WD=Q66890725|1=Tenjin faith}}), which stood at the current site, and it was renamed Ninomiya Shrine<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>.

The name Ninomiya Shrine could be interpreted as deriving from the deity being the second daughter among the Hakone three sisters, but the official explanation says it derives from the second prince who is traditionally said to have accompanied the deity<ref name="kanko"/>.

=== Enshrined deity ===
* Iwanohime-no-Mikoto (いわのひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} (Mishima Daimyōjin).

According to the mythology in ''Miyakeki'', the three sister-consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), enshrined in shrines on Miyake-jima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{ill|lt=Lake Ashi|Q187053|WD=Q187053|1=Lake Ashi}} in {{ill|lt=Hakone|Q11603342|WD=Q11603342|1=Hakone}}, rescued from a dragon deity (great serpent) and brought to Miyake-jima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{ill|lt=Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|WD=Q134930558|lt=Kisaki Shrine|1=Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto enshrined at Ninomiya Shrine, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{ill|lt=Mishaku Shrine|Q110915859|WD=Q110915859|1=Oshaku Shrine}}<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref>.

It stands directly in front of Tsubota fishing port in the southeast of Miyake-jima.

Several {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} and many nearby small shrines are merged here, centered on '''Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' (いわのひめのみことじんじゃ)<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima p.84 (Shikinaisha of Miyakejima)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref>.

It is one of three shrines on Miyake-jima that enshrine the consort of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} (Mishima Daimyōjin)<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html History] - Miyakejima, Tokyo</ref>.

=== History ===
The central Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine originally stood in the northern mountains of the current site (present Iwaha Kannon Shrine / Tsubota Kannon), then in the early Kamakura period moved to southwest of the current site, south of Ōji-ike (Ogura-yama, with torii still extant). Later, in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 29 ({{ill|lt=1954|Q18581|WD=Q18581|1=1954}}), nearby shrines were consolidated into and merged with {{ill|lt=Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247|WD=Q48532247|1=Sugawara Shrine (disambiguation)}} (of {{ill|lt=Tenjin worship|Q66890725|WD=Q66890725|1=Tenjin faith}}), which stood at the current site, and it was renamed Ninomiya Shrine<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>.

The name Ninomiya Shrine could be interpreted as deriving from the deity being the second daughter among the Hakone three sisters, but the official explanation says it derives from the second prince who is traditionally said to have accompanied the deity<ref name="kanko"/>.

== Japanese content ==
{{神社
|名称   = Kisaki Shrine
|画像= {{画像募集中|cat=Tokyo Islands}}
|所在地 = {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}{{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797|1=Miyake, Tokyo}}{{ill|lt=Tsubota Village|Q11425774|WD=Q11425774|lt=Tsubota|1=Tsubota}}
|位置 = {{Coord|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E|display=inline,title}}

{{coord2|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E}}}}
}}
'''Ninomiya Shrine''' (にのみやじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797|1=Miyake, Tokyo}} ({{ill|lt=Miyake-jima|Q1075076|WD=Q1075076|1=Miyake-jima}}), {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}.

It stands directly in front of Tsubota fishing port in the southeast of Miyake-jima.

Several {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} and many nearby small shrines are merged here, centered on '''Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' (いわのひめのみことじんじゃ)<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima p.84 (Shikinaisha of Miyakejima)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref>.

It is one of three shrines on Miyake-jima that enshrine the consort of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} (Mishima Daimyōjin)<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html History] - Miyakejima, Tokyo</ref>.

=== History ===
The central Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine originally stood in the northern mountains of the current site (present Iwaha Kannon Shrine / Tsubota Kannon), then in the early Kamakura period moved to southwest of the current site, south of Ōji-ike (Ogura-yama, with torii still extant). Later, in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 29 ({{ill|lt=1954|Q18581|WD=Q18581|1=1954}}), nearby shrines were consolidated into and merged with {{ill|lt=Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247|WD=Q48532247|1=Sugawara Shrine (disambiguation)}} (of {{ill|lt=Tenjin worship|Q66890725|WD=Q66890725|1=Tenjin faith}}), which stood at the current site, and it was renamed Ninomiya Shrine<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>.

The name Ninomiya Shrine could be interpreted as deriving from the deity being the second daughter among the Hakone three sisters, but the official explanation says it derives from the second prince who is traditionally said to have accompanied the deity<ref name="kanko"/>.

=== Enshrined deity ===
* Iwanohime-no-Mikoto (いわのひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} (Mishima Daimyōjin).

According to the mythology in ''Miyakeki'', the three sister-consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), enshrined in shrines on Miyake-jima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{ill|lt=Lake Ashi|Q187053|WD=Q187053|1=Lake Ashi}} in {{ill|lt=Hakone|Q11603342|WD=Q11603342|1=Hakone}}, rescued from a dragon deity (great serpent) and brought to Miyake-jima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{ill|lt=Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|WD=Q134930558|lt=Kisaki Shrine|1=Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto enshrined at Ninomiya Shrine, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{ill|lt=Mishaku Shrine|Q110915859|WD=Q110915859|1=Oshaku Shrine}}<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref>.

{{nihongo|'''Ninomiya Shrine'''|二宮神社 (三宅村)}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Izu Province]].

{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E}}}}
}}
'''Ninomiya Shrine''' (にのみやじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797|1=Miyake, Tokyo}} ({{ill|lt=Miyake-jima|Q1075076|WD=Q1075076|1=Miyake-jima}}), {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}.

It stands directly in front of Tsubota fishing port in the southeast of Miyake-jima.

Several {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} and many nearby small shrines are merged here, centered on '''Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' (いわのひめのみことじんじゃ)<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima p.84 (Shikinaisha of Miyakejima)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref>.

It is one of three shrines on Miyake-jima that enshrine the consort of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} (Mishima Daimyōjin)<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html History] - Miyakejima, Tokyo</ref>.

=== History ===
The central Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine originally stood in the northern mountains of the current site (present Iwaha Kannon Shrine / Tsubota Kannon), then in the early Kamakura period moved to southwest of the current site, south of Ōji-ike (Ogura-yama, with torii still extant). Later, in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 29 ({{ill|lt=1954|Q18581|WD=Q18581|1=1954}}), nearby shrines were consolidated into and merged with {{ill|lt=Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247|WD=Q48532247|1=Sugawara Shrine (disambiguation)}} (of {{ill|lt=Tenjin worship|Q66890725|WD=Q66890725|1=Tenjin faith}}), which stood at the current site, and it was renamed Ninomiya Shrine<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>.

The name Ninomiya Shrine could be interpreted as deriving from the deity being the second daughter among the Hakone three sisters, but the official explanation says it derives from the second prince who is traditionally said to have accompanied the deity<ref name="kanko"/>.

=== Enshrined deity ===
* Iwanohime-no-Mikoto (いわのひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} (Mishima Daimyōjin).

According to the mythology in ''Miyakeki'', the three sister-consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), enshrined in shrines on Miyake-jima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{ill|lt=Lake Ashi|Q187053|WD=Q187053|1=Lake Ashi}} in {{ill|lt=Hakone|Q11603342|WD=Q11603342|1=Hakone}}, rescued from a dragon deity (great serpent) and brought to Miyake-jima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{ill|lt=Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|WD=Q134930558|lt=Kisaki Shrine|1=Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto enshrined at Ninomiya Shrine, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{ill|lt=Mishaku Shrine|Q110915859|WD=Q110915859|1=Oshaku Shrine}}<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref>.

{{nihongo|'''Ninomiya Shrine'''|二宮神社 (三宅村)}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Izu Province]].

'''Ninomiya Shrine''' (にのみやじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797|1=Miyake, Tokyo}} ({{ill|lt=Miyake-jima|Q1075076|WD=Q1075076|1=Miyake-jima}}), {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}.

It stands directly in front of Tsubota fishing port in the southeast of Miyake-jima.

Several {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} and many nearby small shrines are merged here, centered on '''Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' (いわのひめのみことじんじゃ)<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima p.84 (Shikinaisha of Miyakejima)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref>.

It is one of three shrines on Miyake-jima that enshrine the consort of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} (Mishima Daimyōjin)<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html History] - Miyakejima, Tokyo</ref>.

=== History ===
The central Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine originally stood in the northern mountains of the current site (present Iwaha Kannon Shrine / Tsubota Kannon), then in the early Kamakura period moved to southwest of the current site, south of Ōji-ike (Ogura-yama, with torii still extant). Later, in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 29 ({{ill|lt=1954|Q18581|WD=Q18581|1=1954}}), nearby shrines were consolidated into and merged with {{ill|lt=Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247|WD=Q48532247|1=Sugawara Shrine (disambiguation)}} (of {{ill|lt=Tenjin worship|Q66890725|WD=Q66890725|1=Tenjin faith}}), which stood at the current site, and it was renamed Ninomiya Shrine<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>.

The name Ninomiya Shrine could be interpreted as deriving from the deity being the second daughter among the Hakone three sisters, but the official explanation says it derives from the second prince who is traditionally said to have accompanied the deity<ref name="kanko"/>.

=== Enshrined deity ===
* Iwanohime-no-Mikoto (いわのひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} (Mishima Daimyōjin).

According to the mythology in ''Miyakeki'', the three sister-consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), enshrined in shrines on Miyake-jima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{ill|lt=Lake Ashi|Q187053|WD=Q187053|1=Lake Ashi}} in {{ill|lt=Hakone|Q11603342|WD=Q11603342|1=Hakone}}, rescued from a dragon deity (great serpent) and brought to Miyake-jima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{ill|lt=Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|WD=Q134930558|lt=Kisaki Shrine|1=Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto enshrined at Ninomiya Shrine, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{ill|lt=Mishaku Shrine|Q110915859|WD=Q110915859|1=Oshaku Shrine}}<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref>.

== Japanese content ==
