{{coord2|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E}}}}
}}
'''Ninomiya Shrine''' (にのみやじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797|1=Miyake, Tokyo}} ({{ill|lt=Miyake-jima|Q1075076|WD=Q1075076|1=Miyake-jima}}), {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}.

It stands directly in front of Tsubota fishing port in the southeast of Miyake-jima.

Several {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} and many nearby small shrines are merged here, centered on '''Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' (いわのひめのみことじんじゃ)<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima p.84 (Shikinaisha of Miyakejima)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref>.

It is one of three shrines on Miyake-jima that enshrine the consort of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} (Mishima Daimyōjin)<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html History] - Miyakejima, Tokyo</ref>.

=== History ===
The central Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine originally stood in the northern mountains of the current site (present Iwaha Kannon Shrine / Tsubota Kannon), then in the early Kamakura period moved to southwest of the current site, south of Ōji-ike (Ogura-yama, with torii still extant). Later, in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 29 ({{ill|lt=1954|Q18581|WD=Q18581|1=1954}}), nearby shrines were consolidated into and merged with {{ill|lt=Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247|WD=Q48532247|1=Sugawara Shrine (disambiguation)}} (of {{ill|lt=Tenjin worship|Q66890725|WD=Q66890725|1=Tenjin faith}}), which stood at the current site, and it was renamed Ninomiya Shrine<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>.

The name Ninomiya Shrine could be interpreted as deriving from the deity being the second daughter among the Hakone three sisters, but the official explanation says it derives from the second prince who is traditionally said to have accompanied the deity<ref name="kanko"/>.

=== Enshrined deity ===
* Iwanohime-no-Mikoto (いわのひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} (Mishima Daimyōjin).

According to the mythology in ''Miyakeki'', the three sister-consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), enshrined in shrines on Miyake-jima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{ill|lt=Lake Ashi|Q187053|WD=Q187053|1=Lake Ashi}} in {{ill|lt=Hakone|Q11603342|WD=Q11603342|1=Hakone}}, rescued from a dragon deity (great serpent) and brought to Miyake-jima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{ill|lt=Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|WD=Q134930558|lt=Kisaki Shrine|1=Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto enshrined at Ninomiya Shrine, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{ill|lt=Mishaku Shrine|Q110915859|WD=Q110915859|1=Oshaku Shrine}}<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref>.

'''Ninomiya Shrine''' (にのみやじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797|1=Miyake, Tokyo}} ({{ill|lt=Miyake-jima|Q1075076|WD=Q1075076|1=Miyake-jima}}), {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}.

{{nihongo|'''Ninomiya Shrine'''|二宮神社 (三宅村)}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Izu Province]].

{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|3|37.6|N|139|32|41.4|E}}}}

'''Ninomiya Shrine''' (にのみやじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Miyake Village|Q1154797|WD=Q1154797|1=Miyake, Tokyo}} ({{ill|lt=Miyake-jima|Q1075076|WD=Q1075076|1=Miyake-jima}}), {{ill|lt=Tokyo|Q1490|WD=Q1490|1=Tokyo}}.

It stands directly in front of Tsubota fishing port in the southeast of Miyake-jima.

Several {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} and many nearby small shrines are merged here, centered on '''Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine''' (いわのひめのみことじんじゃ)<ref name="kanko">[https://www.miyakejima.gr.jp/see/ninomiya_shrine/ 二宮神社] - Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref><ref name="chosa">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz140118-02.html 二宮神社] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref><ref name="miyake-84">[https://www.soumu.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/14miyake/miyakehp/kankousasshi_0503.pdf Miyakejima p.84 (Shikinaisha of Miyakejima)] - Tokyo Metropolitan Government / Miyakejima Tourism Association</ref>.

It is one of three shrines on Miyake-jima that enshrine the consort of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} (Mishima Daimyōjin)<ref>[https://www.vill.miyake.tokyo.jp/gyousei/syoukai/history.html History] - Miyakejima, Tokyo</ref>.

=== History ===
The central Iwanohime-no-Mikoto Shrine originally stood in the northern mountains of the current site (present Iwaha Kannon Shrine / Tsubota Kannon), then in the early Kamakura period moved to southwest of the current site, south of Ōji-ike (Ogura-yama, with torii still extant). Later, in {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|WD=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 29 ({{ill|lt=1954|Q18581|WD=Q18581|1=1954}}), nearby shrines were consolidated into and merged with {{ill|lt=Sugawara Shrine|Q48532247|WD=Q48532247|1=Sugawara Shrine (disambiguation)}} (of {{ill|lt=Tenjin worship|Q66890725|WD=Q66890725|1=Tenjin faith}}), which stood at the current site, and it was renamed Ninomiya Shrine<ref name="chosa"/><ref name="miyake-84"/>.

The name Ninomiya Shrine could be interpreted as deriving from the deity being the second daughter among the Hakone three sisters, but the official explanation says it derives from the second prince who is traditionally said to have accompanied the deity<ref name="kanko"/>.

=== Enshrined deity ===
* Iwanohime-no-Mikoto (いわのひめのみこと) - one of the consort deities of {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Kami|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} (Mishima Daimyōjin).

According to the mythology in ''Miyakeki'', the three sister-consorts of Mishima Daimyōjin (Kotoshironushi-no-Kami), enshrined in shrines on Miyake-jima, were daughters of an old couple living around {{ill|lt=Lake Ashi|Q187053|WD=Q187053|1=Lake Ashi}} in {{ill|lt=Hakone|Q11603342|WD=Q11603342|1=Hakone}}, rescued from a dragon deity (great serpent) and brought to Miyake-jima. The eldest is Igamuhime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{ill|lt=Kisaki Shrine (Miyake Village)|Q134930558|WD=Q134930558|lt=Kisaki Shrine|1=Kisaki Shrine}}, the second is Iwanohime-no-Mikoto enshrined at Ninomiya Shrine, and the third is Sakitamahime-no-Mikoto enshrined at {{ill|lt=Mishaku Shrine|Q110915859|WD=Q110915859|1=Oshaku Shrine}}<ref name="chosa-miyake">[http://engishiki.org/izu/bun/iz149900-01.html Shrines of Miyakejima] - Research on Engishiki Shrines</ref>.
